items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: If 50 cm^3 of a 1 mol/dm^3 solution is diluted to 100 cm^3, what is the new concentration?
items.correct: 0.5 mol/dm^3 — the moles stay the same but the volume doubled.
items.misconception: The new concentration is 0.05 mol/dm^3 because you doubled the volume so you halve the concentration once and shift the decimal too.
items.random_wrong: The new concentration is 2 mol/dm^3 because adding water makes the solution stronger.
items.why: Students panic with unit conversions and over-divide, or they confuse 'add water = more concentrated' through a misread of the procedure.

items.q: For the Haber process N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (exothermic), increasing temperature:
items.why: Correct answer: Decreases yield of NH₃. “Increases yield of NH₃” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Adding a catalyst to an equilibrium system:
items.why: It's Reaches equilibrium faster, same position. “Increases yield” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
